schools.summary: Gideon and his host pitching beside the well of Harod, the Midianites to the north: 'then Jerubbaal, who is Gideon, and all the people that were with him, rose up early, and pitched beside the well of Harod: so that the host of the Midianites were on the north side of them, by the hill of Moreh, in the valley' — Gideon mustered his men by the well of Harod, the Midianite host facing them (the then Jerubbaal, who is Gideon, and all the people that were with him, rose up early, and pitched beside the well of Harod, Gideon's camp, the so that the host of the Midianites were on the north side of them, by the hill of Moreh, in the valley, the Midianites to the north, the figure, the camps drawn up, the few of Israel against the great host of Midian, the truth that Gideon, called Jerubbaal for casting down Baal, faced the vast Midianite host, Judges 6:32, the figure of the small band of God's people facing the multitude of the foe, the encampment at Harod, the armies arrayed, vv.6); Gideon pitching by the well of Harod (the camp, Judges 6:32) — the camp (at Harod); 'the host of the Midianites were on the north side of them, by the hill of Moreh' (the Midianite host to the north, the foe arrayed, the figure, the small band against the multitude, the providence, the encampment, God's word that the people were too many to follow, vv.2) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: God's word that the people were too many, lest Israel boast that their own hand had saved them: 'and the LORD said unto Gideon, The people that are with thee are too many for me to give the Midianites into their hands, lest Israel vaunt themselves against me, saying, Mine own hand hath saved me' — the exclusion of boasting — 'and the LORD said unto Gideon, The people that are with thee are too many for me to give the Midianites into their hands, lest Israel vaunt themselves against me, saying, Mine own hand hath saved me': God declares the army too large, that Israel might not claim the victory as their own (the witnesses noting the significance — the deliberate purpose of God to reduce the host so small that the victory could not be ascribed to human strength, that no flesh might glory but the deliverance be owned as wholly his, the army of thirty-two thousand too many, God whittling it down lest the people boast 'mine own hand hath saved me,' the glory of salvation jealously guarded for God, the lesson — that God so orders his deliverances that man cannot claim them, the means made weak that the power be manifestly his, 'that no flesh should glory in his presence,' 1 Corinthians 1:29, the truth that God will not give his glory to another, and so saves by the few and the weak, that the praise be his alone, 'not unto us, O LORD, not unto us, but unto thy name give glory,' Psalm 115:1, the figure — the salvation that is wholly of God and not of man, fulfilled in the gospel where boasting is excluded and grace alone saves, 'where is boasting then? It is excluded,' Romans 3:27, the truth that God strips away every prop of human pride that the deliverance may be confessed as his gift, the figure of God's purpose to exclude all human boasting, that the glory be wholly his, the call to ascribe all our salvation to God and claim no part for ourselves, the warning against the pride that says 'mine own hand hath saved me,' the figure of God's purpose to exclude all human boasting, that the glory of salvation be wholly his); God declaring the host too large to be given the victory, the army to be reduced that the deliverance not be ascribed to numbers (the exclusion of boasting, 1 Corinthians 1:29) — the word (too many); 'lest Israel vaunt themselves against me, saying, Mine own hand hath saved me' (God reducing the host lest Israel boast their own hand had saved them, the glory of salvation guarded for God alone, who saves by the few that the praise be his, the glory given to God, Psalm 115:1; Romans 3:27) — the figure (the salvation wholly of God and not of man, the providence, the reason, the proclamation for the fearful to depart to follow, vv.3) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: Why does God say the people are too many?

sides.position: To exclude all human boasting, that the glory of salvation be wholly his - God's deliberate purpose to reduce the host so small that the victory could not be ascribed to human strength, lest Israel say 'mine own hand hath saved me,' that no flesh might glory (1 Corinthians 1:29), God refusing to give his glory to another (Psalm 115:1), a figure of the gospel where boasting is excluded (Romans 3:27)

schools.summary: The fearful sent home, twenty-two thousand departing: 'now therefore go to, proclaim in the ears of the people, saying, Whosoever is fearful and afraid, let him return and depart early from mount Gilead. schools.summary: God's word that the people were yet too many, to be tried at the water: 'and the LORD said unto Gideon, The people are yet too many; bring them down unto the water, and I will try them for thee there: and it shall be, that of whom I say unto thee, This shall go with thee, the same shall go with thee; and of whomsoever I say unto thee, This shall not go with thee, the same shall not go' — God declared ten thousand still too many and would try them at the water (the and the LORD said unto Gideon, The people are yet too many; bring them down unto the water, and I will try them for thee there, yet too many, the and it shall be, that of whom I say unto thee, This shall go with thee... the same shall not go, God choosing who would go, the figure, the further sifting, God himself choosing the men at the water, the truth that God reduced the host yet more, choosing his own instruments, Judges 7:2, the figure of the God who chooses his instruments, the few selected by his hand, the trial at the water, the further reduction, vv.2); God trying the host at the water (the sifting, Judges 7:2) — the trial (at the water); 'of whom I say unto thee, This shall go with thee... the same shall not go' (God himself choosing the men, the divine choice, the figure, the God who chooses his instruments, the providence, the trial, the lapping at the water to follow, vv.5) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: God's word that by the three hundred alone he would save Israel and deliver Midian: 'and the LORD said unto Gideon, By the three hundred men that lapped will I save you, and deliver the Midianites into thine hand: and let all the other people go every man unto his place' — the salvation by the few — 'and the LORD said unto Gideon, By the three hundred men that lapped will I save you, and deliver the Midianites into thine hand: and let all the other people go every man unto his place': God promises to save by the three hundred alone, sending the rest away (the witnesses noting the significance — the deliberate choice of God to deliver by a tiny band against a host like the sand of the sea, that the victory be manifestly his and not the work of numbers, the three hundred against thousands proving the deliverance God's alone, the host reduced from thirty-two thousand to three hundred, the means made so weak that no power but God's could account for the victory, 'by the alone, the deliverance wrought by a tiny band that it be manifestly his (the salvation by the few, 2 Corinthians 12:9) — the promise (by the three hundred); 'and let all the other people go every man schools.summary: The Midianite's dream of a barley cake overturning the tent: 'and when Gideon was come, behold, there was a man that told a dream unto his fellow, and said, Behold, I dreamed a dream, and, lo, a cake of barley bread tumbled into the host of Midian, and came unto a tent, and smote it that it fell, and overturned it, that the tent lay along' — God ruling the enemy's heart — 'and when schools.summary: Gideon arming the three hundred with trumpets, empty pitchers, and hidden lamps: 'and he divided the three hundred men into three companies, and he put a trumpet in every man's hand, with empty pitchers, and lamps within the pitchers' — the weak instruments — 'and he divided the three hundred men into three companies, and he put a trumpet in every man's hand, with empty pitchers, and lamps within the pitchers': Gideon armed the three hundred not with swords but with trumpets, jars, and torches (the witnesses noting the significance — the strange and lowly weapons by which God would rout the host, no swords or spears but trumpets to sound, empty pitchers to break, and lamps to flash, the victory plainly God's since the means could not in themselves prevail, the three hundred with no carnal weapons but the trumpet, the broken jar, and the light, that the deliverance be ascribed to God alone, the foolishness of the means magnifying the power of God, the lesson — that God works his greatest deliverances by the weakest and most unlikely means, the power resting not in the instrument but in him, 'we have this treasure in earthen vessels, that the excellency of the power may be of God, and not of us,' 2 Corinthians 4:7, the truth that God chooses the weak and the foolish things, the trumpet and the broken pitcher, to confound the mighty, that no flesh should glory, 'the weakness of God is stronger than men,' 1 Corinthians 1:25, the figure — the gospel ministry, weak in itself yet mighty through God to the pulling down of strongholds, the earthen vessel that holds the light, the trumpet of the word and the broken vessel that lets the light shine, 'the light of the knowledge of the glory of God,' 2 Corinthians 4:6, the truth that the breaking of the vessel let the light blaze forth, the weakness the very means of the power, the figure of the weak and unlikely means by which God works his victory, the power not in the instrument but in him, the call to be content to be weak vessels for God's light, the assurance that God's power shines through broken and lowly means, the figure of the weak and unlikely means by which God works his victory, the power not in the instrument but in him); Gideon dividing the three hundred into three companies to surround the host, the small band arrayed for God's victory (the weak band, 2 Corinthians 4:7) — the division (three companies); 'he put a trumpet in every man's hand, with empty pitchers, and lamps within the pitchers' (the men armed with trumpets, empty pitchers, and hidden lamps, no carnal weapons, the victory plainly God's since the means could not prevail, the weak things confounding the mighty, the earthen vessel holding the light, the power of God in earthen vessels, 1 Corinthians 1:25; 2 Corinthians 4:6) — the figure (the gospel ministry weak in itself yet mighty through God, the providence, the means, the charge to watch and do as Gideon to follow, vv.17) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What do the trumpets, empty pitchers, and lamps signify?

sides.position: The weak and unlikely means by which God works his victory, the power not in the instrument but in him - the strange and lowly weapons by which God routed the host, no swords but trumpet, broken jar, and light, the victory plainly God's, the power not in the instrument but in him (2 Corinthians 4:7), the weak confounding the mighty (1 Corinthians 1:25), a figure of the gospel treasure in earthen vessels broken that the light may blaze (2 Corinthians 4:6)

themes: And he divided the three hundred men into three companies, and he put a trumpet in every man's hand, with empty pitchers, and lamps within the pitchers

God working his greatest deliverances by the weakest means, the power not in the instrument but in him, a figure of the gospel treasure in earthen vessels (2 Corinthians 4:7; 1 Corinthians 1:25; 2 Corinthians 4:6)

schools.summary: Gideon charging the three hundred to watch and do as he did: 'and he said unto them, Look on me, and do likewise: and, behold, when I come to the outside of the camp, it shall be that, as I do, so shall ye do' — Gideon bade the men watch him and do the same (the and he said unto them, Look on me, and do likewise, look and do likewise, the and, behold, when I come to the outside of the camp, it shall be that, as I do, so shall ye do, do as Gideon does, the figure, the leader's example, the men to follow his act exactly, the truth that Gideon set himself as the pattern, the men to imitate, Judges 7:18, the figure of the leader who goes first and bids his men follow his example, 'be ye followers of me,' 1 Corinthians 11:1, the charge to imitate, the example of the leader, vv.16); Gideon setting himself as the pattern (the example, 1 Corinthians 11:1) — the example (look on me); 'as I do, so shall ye do' (the men to do exactly as Gideon, the imitation, the figure, the leader who goes first and bids his men follow, the providence, the charge, the trumpets and the cry to follow, vv.18) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Gideon's charge to blow the trumpets and cry, The sword of the LORD and of Gideon: 'when I blow with a trumpet, I and all that are with me, then blow ye the trumpets also on every side of all the camp, and say, The sword of the LORD, and of Gideon' — at Gideon's signal all were to blow and cry the battle cry (the when I blow with a trumpet, I and all that are with me, then blow ye the trumpets also on every side of all the camp, blow on every side, the and say, The sword of the LORD, and of Gideon, the battle cry, the figure, the trumpets on every side and the cry, the terror prepared, the LORD and his servant joined, the truth that the cry joined the LORD and Gideon, the victory God's and the means his servant's, Judges 7:20, the figure of the battle cry that owns God's hand and his instrument, the sword of the LORD and of Gideon, the charge to cry, the battle cry prepared, vv.17); the trumpets to be blown on every side (the signal, Judges 7:20) — the trumpets (on every side); 'The sword of the LORD, and of Gideon' (the battle cry joining the LORD and his servant, the cry, the figure, the cry that owns God's hand and his instrument, the providence, the charge, the trumpets blown and the pitchers broken to follow, vv.19-20) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Gideon and his company reaching the camp and blowing the trumpets, breaking the pitchers: 'so Gideon, and the hundred men that were with him, came unto the outside of the camp in the beginning of the middle watch; and they had but newly set the watch: and they blew the trumpets, and brake the pitchers that were in their hands' — Gideon's company came at the watch and blew and broke the pitchers (the so Gideon, and the hundred men that were with him, came unto the outside of the camp in the beginning of the middle watch; and they had but newly set the watch, the onset at the watch, the and they blew the trumpets, and brake the pitchers that were in their hands, blew and broke the pitchers, the figure, the sudden onset at the changing of the watch, the trumpets and the broken pitchers, the surprise, the truth that Gideon struck at the changing of the watch, the foe surprised, Judges 7:20, the figure of the surprise that God times, the broken vessel and the trumpet at the moment of confusion, the onset of Gideon, the surprise, vv.18); Gideon striking at the changing of the watch (the surprise, Judges 7:20) — the timing (the middle watch); 'they blew the trumpets, and brake the pitchers that were in their hands' (the trumpets blown and pitchers broken, the onset, the figure, the surprise that God times, the providence, the onset, the cry and the rout to follow, vv.20-22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: So Gideon, and the hundred men that were with him, came unto the outside of the camp in the beginning of the middle watch; and they had but newly set the watch: and they blew the trumpets, and brake the pitchers that were in their hands

schools.summary: The three companies crying, The sword of the LORD and of Gideon, as they broke the pitchers and held the lamps: 'and the three companies blew the trumpets, and brake the pitchers, and held the lamps in their left hands, and the trumpets in their right hands to blow withal: and they cried, The sword of the LORD, and of Gideon' — the sword of the LORD and of Gideon — 'and the three companies blew the trumpets, and brake the pitchers, and held the lamps in their left hands, and the trumpets in their right hands to blow withal: and they cried, The sword of the LORD, and of Gideon': the three hundred broke the jars, raised the torches, blew the trumpets, and cried the battle cry (the witnesses noting the significance — the joining of the LORD and his servant in the cry, the victory wrought by God yet through the appointed instrument, neither the sword of the LORD without Gideon nor of Gideon without the LORD, the divine power and the human means united, the cry ascribing the deliverance first and chiefly to the LORD, 'the sword of the LORD,' yet owning the instrument he used, 'and of Gideon,' the broken vessel and the light a parable of the victory, the light hid till the vessel broke and then blazing forth, the lesson — that God works his victories through his servants, the power his and the instrument theirs, the deliverance owned as wholly his yet wrought by the means he appoints, 'we are labourers together with God,' 1 Corinthians 3:9, the truth that the believer is to act in dependence on God, joining his utmost effort to entire reliance on the LORD, the trumpet blown and the cry raised in faith, 'the LORD wrought a great victory,' 1 Samuel 19:5, the figure — the warfare of the church, the sword of the Spirit which is the word of God wielded by his servants, the light of the gospel shining from broken vessels, 'the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God,' Ephesians 6:17, the truth that the breaking of the vessels let the light shine and the cry go forth, the weakness owning the power, the figure of the victory ascribed jointly to God and his instrument, the divine power working through the human servant, the call to join our effort to God's power and ascribe the victory to him, the assurance that God conquers through his weak and faithful servants, the figure of the victory ascribed jointly to God and his instrument, the divine power working through the human servant); the three companies breaking the jars and raising the torches, the light blazing forth from the broken vessels at the trumpet blast (the light from broken vessels, 1 Corinthians 3:9) — the act (broke the pitchers, held the lamps); 'they cried, The sword of the LORD, and of Gideon' (the cry joining the LORD and his servant, the victory ascribed first to God yet owning the instrument, the believer joining effort to reliance, the sword of the Spirit wielded by God's servants, the power of God and the instrument joined, 1 Samuel 19:5; Ephesians 6:17) — the figure (the warfare of the church, the word of God wielded by his servants, the providence, the cry, the host fleeing and crying to follow, vv.21-22) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the cry 'The sword of the LORD, and of Gideon' signify?

sides.position: The victory ascribed jointly to God and his instrument, the divine power working through the human servant - neither the sword of the LORD without Gideon nor of Gideon without the LORD, the deliverance owned as wholly God's yet wrought by the means he appoints (1 Corinthians 3:9), the believer joining effort to reliance (1 Samuel 19:5), a figure of the church's warfare with the sword of the Spirit (Ephesians 6:17)

the deliverance owned as wholly God's yet wrought by the means he appoints, a figure of the church's warfare with the sword of the Spirit shining from broken vessels (1 Corinthians 3:9; 1 Samuel 19:5; Ephesians 6:17)

schools.summary: Israel standing still while the host ran, cried, and fled: 'and they stood every man in his place round about the camp: and all the host ran, and cried, and fled' — the three hundred stood still while the host fled in panic (the and they stood every man in his place round about the camp, Israel stood still, the and all the host ran, and cried, and fled, the host fled, the figure, Israel standing still while God routed the foe, the victory his alone, the truth that the three hundred did not strike but stood, and the host fled, the victory God's, Exodus 14:13, the figure of the salvation of God that the people have only to stand still and see, the foe routed without their sword, the standing of Israel, the flight of the host, vv.20); the three hundred standing still (the stillness, Exodus 14:13) — the standing (every man in his place); 'all the host ran, and cried, and fled' (the host fleeing in panic, the rout, the figure, the salvation of God the people have only to stand still and see, the providence, the rout, the LORD setting every man's sword against his fellow to follow, vv.22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: That the LORD set every man's sword against his fellow throughout the host: 'and the three hundred blew the trumpets, and the LORD set every man's sword against his fellow, even throughout all the host: and the host fled to Bethshittah in Zererath, and to the border of Abelmeholah, unto Tabbath' — the foe self-destroyed — 'and the three hundred blew the trumpets, and the LORD set every man's sword against his fellow, even throughout all the host: and the host fled': as the three hundred blew, God turned the Midianites' swords against one another (the witnesses noting the significance — the sovereign hand of God working the destruction of his enemies by their own hand, the panic and darkness causing the host to slay one another, the strength and weapons of the foe turned to their own ruin, the deliverance wrought without a blow from Israel, the LORD himself setting their swords against their fellows, the great host destroying itself in the confusion, the victory beyond all doubt his, the lesson — that God can destroy his enemies by their own strength and weapons, turning their might against themselves, 'they shall fall every one by the sword of his brother,' that the foe needs no other destroyer than himself when God so wills, 'the LORD set ambushments... and they were smitten,' 2 Chronicles 20:22, the truth that God overthrows the wicked by their own devices, their counsel turned to confusion and their swords to mutual slaughter, 'he taketh the wise in their own craftiness,' Job 5:13, the figure — the self-destruction of the kingdom of darkness, divided against itself and falling, 'if a kingdom be divided against itself, that kingdom cannot stand,' Mark 3:24, the enemies of God's people destroying one another, the truth that the victory was so manifestly God's that Israel had but to blow the trumpet and stand, the figure of God turning the strength of his enemies against themselves, destroying them by their own hand, the call to trust the God who needs no arm of flesh, the assurance that God can ruin the foe by the foe's own sword, the figure of God turning the strength of his enemies against themselves, destroying them by their own hand); God turning the Midianites' swords against one another throughout the host as the trumpets blew, the foe destroying itself in the panic (the self-destruction, 2 Chronicles 20:22) — the rout (every man's sword against his fellow); 'and the host fled to Bethshittah in Zererath, and to the border of Abelmeholah, unto Tabbath' (the host fleeing in confusion, the strength of the foe turned to its own ruin, God overthrowing the wicked by their own devices, the kingdom divided against itself falling, the foe ruined by its own hand, Job 5:13; Mark 3:24) — the figure (the self-destruction of the kingdom of darkness divided against itself, the providence, the rout, Israel gathered to pursue to follow, vv.23) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the LORD setting every man's sword against his fellow signify?

sides.position: God turning the strength of his enemies against themselves, destroying them by their own hand - the sovereign hand of God working the destruction of his enemies by their own hand, the host slaying one another in the panic, the deliverance wrought without a blow from Israel, God destroying his enemies by their own strength (2 Chronicles 20:22), overthrowing the wicked by their own devices (Job 5:13), the kingdom divided against itself falling (Mark 3:24)

schools.summary: Israel gathering from the tribes to pursue Midian: 'and the men of Israel gathered themselves together out of Naphtali, and out of Asher, and out of all Manasseh, and pursued after the Midianites' — the men dismissed before were now gathered to pursue (the and the men of Israel gathered themselves together out of Naphtali, and out of Asher, and out of all Manasseh, the tribes gathered, the and pursued after the Midianites, pursued Midian, the figure, the dismissed men now joining the pursuit, the victory shared after God had wrought it, the truth that the rest of Israel, dismissed before the battle, now pursued the fleeing foe, Judges 7:3, the figure of the people joining to complete the victory God had begun, the pursuit of the routed foe, the gathering of Israel, the pursuit, vv.3); the tribes gathering to pursue (the muster, Judges 7:3) — the gathering (the tribes); 'pursued after the Midianites' (Israel pursuing the fleeing foe, the pursuit, the figure, the people joining to complete the victory God had begun, the providence, the pursuit, the Ephraimites taking the waters and the princes to follow, vv.24-25) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Gideon calling Ephraim to take the waters of Jordan: 'and Gideon sent messengers throughout all mount Ephraim, saying, Come down against the Midianites, and take before them the waters unto Bethbarah and Jordan.
